Name: v1: NakedClaude Demo
Description: Rulebook generated from Airtable base 'v1: NakedClaude Demo'.

schema.name: CurrentColor
schema.Description: The current color assigned to this customer (e.g. red, green, yellow, blue)
schema.explanation_rich: **The one raw input that drives everything else in this rulebook.** `CurrentColor` is just a string — `Red`, `Green`, `Yellow`, `Blue`. The whole v1 demo exists to show that one downstream calculated field reads this one raw field. Edit a customer's color and the approval flag follows in every substrate on the next read.

schema.name: IsApproved
schema.explanation_rich: **The smallest demonstrable claim of the whole methodology.** `IsApproved` is `TRUE` when `CurrentColor` is exactly `Green`, otherwise `FALSE` — a single `IF` over a single raw field. The point is not the rule; the point is *where the rule lives*. It lives in the rulebook, not in application code. Worked example: **Bob** has `CurrentColor = Green`, so `IsApproved = TRUE`. **Alice Johnson** has `CurrentColor = Red`, so `IsApproved = FALSE`. Change Alice's color to `Green` and the Postgres view, the Python module, and the Excel cell all flip her to approved on the next read — no glue code, no migration.

Customers.summary_rich: **One table, one rule.** Each customer carries a `CurrentColor` and an `IsApproved` flag that the rulebook derives from it. Flip a customer's color to `Green` and they are approved — in every substrate, the same instant, with no application code.

_meta.tagline: One table, one rule: turn a customer green and they're approved — in every substrate.
_meta.description_rich: NakedClaude v1 is the **first rung of a four-rung ladder** and the smallest claim the methodology can make. A single `Customers` table with one raw field (`CurrentColor`) and one calculated field (`IsApproved = IF(CurrentColor="Green", TRUE, FALSE)`). That's it — no relationships, no lookups, no aggregations. The point is that even *this* trivial rule lives in the rulebook, and Postgres, Python, and Excel all compute it identically without anyone writing application code. v2 adds a related `Statuses` table and lookups; v3 and v4 climb further. Start here to see what the floor looks like before the ladder rises.
use_cases: **Approve Alice.** Change `alice-johnson.CurrentColor` from `Red` to `Green` and watch `IsApproved` flip to `TRUE` on the next read of every substrate.
**Try to break the rule.** Set `caroline.CurrentColor` to `green` (lowercase) — `IsApproved` stays `FALSE`, because the rule is case-sensitive. The rulebook is the only place to change that.
**Edit the rule, not the code.** Swap `="Green"` for `IN("Green","Blue")` in the formula; rebuild; every substrate now approves blue customers too, with zero application changes.
**Compare against v2.** Open `nakedclaude-v2` next door — same `Customers` shape, plus a `Statuses` lookup. v1 → v2 is one step up the ladder.
_meta.journal_seed: Five customers, one of them green. The rule is one line; the demo is the fact that one line is enough.
_meta._CMCC_Summary: Airtable export with schema-first type mapping: Schemas, Data, Relationships (FK links), Lookups (INDEX/MATCH), Aggregations (SUMIFS/COUNTIFS/Rollups), and Calculated fields (formulas) in Excel dialect. Field types are determined from Airtable's schema metadata FIRST (no coercion), with intelligent fallback to formula/data analysis only when schema is unavailable.

_conversion_metadata.field_type_mapping: checkbox→boolean, number→number/integer, multipleRecordLinks→relationship, multipleLookupValues→lookup, rollup→aggregation, count→aggregation, formula→calculated

type_inference.priority: airtable_metadata (NO COERCION) → formula_analysis → reference_resolution → data_analysis (fallback only)
type_inference.airtable_type_mapping: checkbox→boolean, singleLineText→string, multilineText→string, number→number/integer, datetime→datetime, singleSelect→string, email→string, url→string, phoneNumber→string
type_inference.data_coercion_hierarchy: Only used as fallback when Airtable schema unavailable: datetime → number → integer → boolean → base64 → json → string
